content.full_text:
City of Newman
1200 'O' Street • P.O. Box 787 • Newman, CA 95360 • (209) 862-3725

March 19, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

Re: REQUEST FOR FORMAL WRITTEN ADVICE

The City Council of the City of Newman has asked for formal written
advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

The following is submitted for your consideration:

There is presently before the City Council, or will soon come before the
Council, a request to rezone certain lands within the City to Commercial,
or C-1 District, from Residential, and to prezone contiguous lands
presently just outside the City boundaries, also to Commercial or C-1
District, and to prezone and rezone a designated portion of these lands
to Residential or R-1 District. It is contemplated that a request to
annex a portion now outside the City will soon follow; that question is
not formally before the Council at this time.

A portion of these lands, both inside and outside the City, have divided
ownership, with a local real estate broker owning an undivided one-half
interest in a parcel, an undivided one-half interest along with several
other persons, in a parcel, and an option to purchase still another
parcel. The commercial zoning is requested because the contemplated use
of the lands is a shopping center, and the broker will be handling property
as broker for all interests.

The wife of a Councilman is a real estate agent working out of the broker's
office. She does not handle any commercial sales or transactions at all; she
never has and it is not contemplated that she will. She handles only residential sales and transactions and is paid only on a commission basis; she
does not receive a salary or wage. In addition to the agent-broker relationship, the two families are good friends.

Our concerns are twofold:

1. Does the Councilman, by reason of his spouse's professional
relationship with the broker, have a conflict with regard to
the present project of zoning and rezoning and any other
matters that might come up in connection with this specific project?

2. Does the Councilman, by reason of his spouse's professional

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 19, 1990
Page 2.


relationship with the broker, have a conflict in connection with any
other matters at all which may come before the council in which the
broker may be involved?

May the Councilman participate in matters concerning the present project, and
in other matters coming before the Council involving the broker?

Should you have any further questions, please contact me.

Thanking you, I am,

Very truly yours,

[signature]
HAROLD R. DENSMORE
City Attorney
P. O. Box 51
Newman, CA  95360


HRD:cc

cc:  Stephen Hollister, City Manager

follow-up 90-298
JWD

CASSIDY & VERGES
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
ONE ECKER BUILDING, SUITE 210
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94105
TELECOPIER (415) 495-0289
TELEPHONE (415) 243-4444

Mar 20 347 AM '90

March 23, 1990


The Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for formal written advice
made by Mr. Harold R. Densmore,
City Attorney, City of Newman, California

Ladies and Gentlemen:

This firm represents RRP Development Company, Inc.

("RRP") in connection with a real estate development project (the

"Project") presently before, or soon to come before, the City

Council of Newman, California (the "City Council").

RRP has learned that one member of the City Council has

raised the issue of a potential conflict of interest for

Councilman Rodriguez, based on the fact that his wife is employed

by Stephens Realtors, the firm acting as broker in the Project.

The specific issue is whether Councilman Rodriguez has a

"financial interest" in the Project and thus should be prohibited

from participating in land use and other decisions regarding the

Project.

FACTS.

The facts in this situation are accurately described in

the March 19, 1990 request for formal advice made by Mr. Densmore

to this Commission. To summarize, the pertinent facts are as

March 23, 1990
Page 2


follows.  Mrs. Rodriguez is a real estate agent employed -- as an

independent contractor -- by Stephens Realty.  However, she is

not involved in the Project, nor does she ever handle commercial

transactions.  She handles only residential transactions.  More

importantly, Mrs. Rodriguez is paid purely on a commission basis,

and does not receive a salary or other wage from Stephens Realty.

For the reasons discussed below, RRP and I believe that

Councilman Rodriguez does not have a conflict of interest in the

present situation.

APPLICABLE STATUTES.

     The statutes applicable to this situation are

Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.  Section 87100 reads as

follows:

     No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in
     any way attempt to use his official position to
     influence a governmental decision in which he
     knows or has reason to know he has a financial
     interest.

     Section 87103 reads, in pertinent part, as follows:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
     reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
     a material financial effect, distinguishable from
     its effect on the public generally, on the
     official or a member of his or her immediate
     family ...

PLAIN LANGUAGE OF THE STATUTE.

     Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial

interest "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will

March 23, 1990
Page 3


have a material financial effect ... on the official or a member

of his or her immediate family ...." As mentioned above,

Mrs. Rodriguez works purely on a commission basis. Because she

is not involved in the Project, she will not receive any

compensation as a result of the consummation of the transactions

involved in the Project. Moreover, because Mrs. Rodriguez works

on a commission basis, any financial benefit derived by Stephens

Realtors from the Project would not inure to her benefit --

Mrs. Rodriguez gets paid only if she sells houses. This is very

different from the situation of a salaried employee, who might

derive some indirect benefit by virtue of the fact that her

employer has realized a direct economic benefit and, as a result,

might theoretically be in a better position to continue to pay

(or even increase) her salary.

MARIN MUNICIPAL WATER DISTRICT OPINION.

        There are some parallels between the present situation

and the situation in which this Commission opined in the matter

of Board of Directors, Marin Municipal Water District, 1 F.P.P.C.

Opinions 198 (No. 75-089 December 4, 1975). In that matter, the

issue was whether certain members of the Board of Directors of

the Marin Municipal Water District (the "MMWD") could participate

in MMWD decisions regarding requests for variances from a

moratorium on new water connections, as well as the possible

decision to lift the moratorium. One issue was whether one of

the Directors had a conflict of interest due to the fact that her

March 23, 1990
Page 4


husband was a project engineer with Dinwiddie Construction

Company, which could conceivably benefit by obtaining

construction jobs in Marin if the moratorium were lifted. In

that matter, this Commission noted that the Director's husband

was paid "on a straight salary basis and would not receive any

additional compensation if Dinwiddie were to obtain a new

contract within the MMWD." (Opinion, Page 4.)

In interpreting Section 87103, this Commission observed

that:

     [S]everal elements must be present before a public
     official is required to disqualify himself from
     participation in a governmental decision. First,
     it must be reasonably foreseeable that the
     governmental decision will have a financial
     effect. Second, the anticipated financial effect
     must be on a financial interest of the official,
     as defined in Sections 87103(a) through (d).
     Third, the anticipated financial effect must be
     material. And fourth, the governmental decision's
     anticipated financial effect on the official's
     financial interest must be distinguishable from
     its effect on the public generally.

(Opinion, Page 4.) In addition, citing People v. Darby, 114

Cal.App.2d 412, 433 (1952), this Commission stated that: "the

ultimate test is whether the element of foreseeability, together

with the other elements discussed earlier, is present to the

point that the official's 'unqualified devotion to his public

duty' might be impaired." (Opinion, Page 9.)

     In discussing the reasons that the Director did not

have a conflict of interest, this Commission noted that any

March 23, 1990
Page 5


benefit realized by her husband's employer would not bear any

relation to her income. This Commission noted that:

     [N]or would any benefit to Dinwiddie necessarily
     bear any relation to Director Lloyd's income from
     Dinwiddie, a factor we believe may be considered
     in determining whether there may be a material
     financial effect on a source of income ... He is
     paid on a straight salary basis and his
     compensation would not be affected by Dinwiddie's
     obtaining or failing to obtain new contracts in
     the MMWD.

(Opinion, Page 11.) As in the MMWD matter, Councilman Rodriguez

has no "financial interest" in any decisions relating to the

Project, because his wife's income would be unaffected by any of

the City Council's decisions relating to the Project.

     For the reasons discussed above, we believe that

Council Rodriguez does not have a conflict of interest, and thus

should be allowed to vote and otherwise participate in all

decisions and other matters pertaining to the Project.  Thank you

for your consideration.

                                    Very truly yours,

                                    [signature]
                                    Thomas E. Panelli

TEP:bam

cc:  Harold R. Densmore, Esq.
     Mr. Russell R. Pratt
     Mr. J. Fred Goldsmith
